title: S.J. Tucker

infobox.value: (born February 22, 1980) is an [Arkansas](/wiki/Arkansas "Arkansas")-born [North American](/wiki/North_American "North American") singer-songwriter. Originally inspired by [alternative](/wiki/Alternative_rock "Alternative rock") [folk rock](/wiki/Folk_rock "Folk rock") artists like [Joni Mitchell](/wiki/Joni_Mitchell "Joni Mitchell"), [Jeff Buckley](/wiki/Jeff_Buckley "Jeff Buckley") and [Ani DiFranco](/wiki/Ani_DiFranco "Ani DiFranco"),[[1]](#cite_note-1) Tucker – also called "Sooj" or "Skinny White Chick" – soon branched out to assume a more diverse identity. Like [The Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles") – another cited influence[[2]](#cite_note-2) – Tucker prefers an eclectic approach to songcraft. Since her debut album in 2004, Tucker's work has integrated elements of [electronica](/wiki/Electronica "Electronica"), [filk](/wiki/Filk "Filk"), [spoken word](/wiki/Spoken_word "Spoken word"), [world music](/wiki/World_music "World music"), [industrial metal](/wiki/Industrial_metal "Industrial metal"), and – with the troupe [Fire & Strings](/w/index.php?title=Fire_%26_Strings&action=edit&redlink=1 "Fire & Strings (page does not exist)") – [fire-spinning](/wiki/Fire-spinning "Fire-spinning").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [S.J. Tucker](/w/index.php?title=S.J._Tucker&redirect=no "S.J. Tucker"))

American singer-songwriter

| S. J. Tucker | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Background information | |

| Born | Susan Jane Tucker   (1980-02-22) February 22, 1980 (age 46) |
| Genres | Rock, [folk](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") |
| Occupations | Musician, singer-songwriter, performance artist |

Musical artist

**S. J. Tucker** (born February 22, 1980) is an [Arkansas](/wiki/Arkansas "Arkansas")-born [North American](/wiki/North_American "North American") singer-songwriter. Originally inspired by [alternative](/wiki/Alternative_rock "Alternative rock") [folk rock](/wiki/Folk_rock "Folk rock") artists like [Joni Mitchell](/wiki/Joni_Mitchell "Joni Mitchell"), [Jeff Buckley](/wiki/Jeff_Buckley "Jeff Buckley") and [Ani DiFranco](/wiki/Ani_DiFranco "Ani DiFranco"),[[1]](#cite_note-1) Tucker – also called "Sooj" or "Skinny White Chick" – soon branched out to assume a more diverse identity. Like [The Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles") – another cited influence[[2]](#cite_note-2) – Tucker prefers an eclectic approach to songcraft. Since her debut album in 2004, Tucker's work has integrated elements of [electronica](/wiki/Electronica "Electronica"), [filk](/wiki/Filk "Filk"), [spoken word](/wiki/Spoken_word "Spoken word"), [world music](/wiki/World_music "World music"), [industrial metal](/wiki/Industrial_metal "Industrial metal"), and – with the troupe [Fire & Strings](/w/index.php?title=Fire_%26_Strings&action=edit&redlink=1 "Fire & Strings (page does not exist)") – [fire-spinning](/wiki/Fire-spinning "Fire-spinning").

## Musical style

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S._J._Tucker&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Musical style")]

Based around the "guitar [troubadour](/wiki/Troubadour "Troubadour")" model, Tucker's work originally featured acoustic guitar, [multitracked](/wiki/Multitrack_recording "Multitrack recording") vocals, low-key bass guitar and [hand drum](/wiki/Hand_drum "Hand drum") parts. In concert, Tucker often appears with a [hollow-body guitar](/wiki/Hollow-body_guitar "Hollow-body guitar") and a [microphone](/wiki/Microphone "Microphone"). However, her third album, *Sirens*, featured [backup](/wiki/Backup "Backup") musicians, a diversity of musical approaches and elaborate vocal [harmonies](/wiki/Harmonies "Harmonies"). Subsequent releases have favored a world [fusion](/wiki/Fusion_(music) "Fusion (music)") approach over guitar [folk](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") arrangements. Stylistically, Tucker has been compared to [Ani DiFranco](/wiki/Ani_DiFranco "Ani DiFranco"), [Fiona Apple](/wiki/Fiona_Apple "Fiona Apple"),[[3]](#cite_note-3) and [Joni Mitchell](/wiki/Joni_Mitchell "Joni Mitchell"),[[4]](#cite_note-4) among other singer-songwriters.

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S._J._Tucker&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

Raised in an art-oriented family, Tucker began singing during childhood. Disgusted by "mainstream" employment, she began performing as a singer and guitarist while at college in 1997, formed her first band, [Skinny White Chick](/w/index.php?title=Skinny_White_Chick&action=edit&redlink=1 "Skinny White Chick (page does not exist)"), in 1999, recording one self-produced EP over the subsequent years. Tucker, who identifies as [bisexual](/wiki/Bisexual "Bisexual"), also found a home in the Gay and Lesbian community, performing monthly at the [Memphis, Tennessee](/wiki/Memphis,_Tennessee "Memphis, Tennessee") Gay and Lesbian Community Center.[[5]](#cite_note-qsaltlake1-5)

After performing as a featured artist at a string of festivals in 2002, Tucker recorded an EP called *Skinny & the Semi-pros* with the members of Memphis band Stout. Collecting funds from the sale of that EP and from concert earnings, Tucker next decided to self-produce her first LP. The result, *Haphazard*, was recorded in the studio over a handful of days and released on March 27, 2004. That album was later cited as one of the "albums no Pagan should be without" in [newWitch](/w/index.php?title=NewWitch&action=edit&redlink=1 "NewWitch (page does not exist)") magazine.[[6]](#cite_note-6) From then on, Tucker began touring full-time, living on the road, in hotel rooms, and in space provided by friends, managers and fans.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In 2004, Tucker met fire-spinner Kevin Wiley during an outdoor festival in Colorado. After a minor accident in which Wiley "hit her in the eye with a flaming ball of Kevlar",[[8]](#cite_note-8) the two became lovers, partners and fellow performers. Combining their respective skills and talents, the couple formed the [Fire & Strings](/w/index.php?title=Fire_%26_Strings&action=edit&redlink=1 "Fire & Strings (page does not exist)") troupe in 2004, and began performing at the [Burning Man](/wiki/Burning_Man "Burning Man") festival and across the United States in 2005.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Since beginning her career, Tucker has recorded, appeared and performed with numerous writers, musicians, activists and bands. Sharing stages with [Phyllis Curott](/wiki/Phyllis_Curott "Phyllis Curott"), [Gaia Consort](/wiki/Gaia_Consort "Gaia Consort"), [Wendy Rule](/wiki/Wendy_Rule "Wendy Rule"), [Selena Fox](/wiki/Selena_Fox "Selena Fox"), [Catherynne M. Valente](/wiki/Catherynne_M._Valente "Catherynne M. Valente"), [Incus](/wiki/Incus "Incus"), Emerald Rose, Rev. [Barry W. Lynn](/wiki/Barry_W._Lynn "Barry W. Lynn"), [Alexander James Adams](/wiki/Alexander_James_Adams "Alexander James Adams"), [SONA](/wiki/SONA_(band) "SONA (band)"), [Celia](/w/index.php?title=Celia_(musician)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Celia (musician) (page does not exist)"), and many others, Tucker has incorporated elements of social activism, [neopagan](/wiki/Neopagan "Neopagan") spirituality and [mythpunk](/wiki/Mythpunk "Mythpunk") fantasy into her repertoire. A 2006 rally in Washington to protest the [Sgt. Patrick Stewart](/wiki/Sgt._Patrick_Stewart "Sgt. Patrick Stewart") case[[10]](#cite_note-10) was later portrayed in the song "Mandolin Holy Man."[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) More recently, Tucker performed several songs from her [neopagan](/wiki/Neopagan "Neopagan") album *Blessings* at the 2007 [Samhain](/wiki/Samhain "Samhain") festival in [Salem, Massachusetts](/wiki/Salem,_Massachusetts "Salem, Massachusetts").[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S._J._Tucker&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Awards")]

Tucker was nominated for both the Best Performer and Best Writer/Composer [Pegasus Awards](/wiki/Pegasus_Award "Pegasus Award") for 2010. She won the Best Performer award, finishing behind Heather Dale for the Best Writer/Composer award. Tucker won the Pegasus Award for Best Writer/Composer in 2011.

## Collaborations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S._J._Tucker&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Collaborations")]

Aside from appearances on albums by [Gaia Consort](/wiki/Gaia_Consort "Gaia Consort"), [Incus](/wiki/Incus "Incus") and [Celia](/w/index.php?title=Celia_(musician)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Celia (musician) (page does not exist)"), Tucker has collaborated on several book/CD crossovers with author [Catherynne M. Valente](/wiki/Catherynne_M._Valente "Catherynne M. Valente"). Inspired by Valente's novel *[The Orphan's Tales: In the Night Garden](/wiki/The_Orphan%27s_Tales:_In_the_Night_Garden "The Orphan's Tales: In the Night Garden")*, Tucker recorded the album *For the Girl in the Garden* and the songs "The Girl in the Garden" and "Shipful of Monsters" as "official companions" to that novel. The two also collaborated on the song "The Drowning" from *Sirens*, and–with K Wiley – performed selections from the novel at various fantasy [conventions](/wiki/Fan_convention "Fan convention"). Tucker's [fire-spinning](/wiki/Fire-spinning "Fire-spinning") inspired Valente to include a fantasy version of her–the Fire-Dancer[[15]](#cite_note-15) – in the 2007 follow-up novel *[The Orphan's Tales: In the Cities of Coin and Spice](/wiki/The_Orphan%27s_Tales:_In_the_Cities_of_Coin_and_Spice "The Orphan's Tales: In the Cities of Coin and Spice")*.[[16]](#cite_note-16) This, in turn, inspired Tucker's song "Firebird’s Child" on the album *Blessings*, released that same year. The two artists further collaborated on the album *Solace & Sorrow*, released in conjunction with *In the Cities of Coin and Spice*, on the same publication day.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Tucker and Valente toured the East Coast together in 2007 to promote Tucker's albums and *The Orphan's Tales*.[[5]](#cite_note-qsaltlake1-5)

Another collaboration of sorts appeared by way of "The Wendy Trilogy" –"Wendy on Board," "Red-Handed Jill" and "Green-Eyed Sue/Sue's Jig." A three-part saga inspired by [J. M. Barrie](/wiki/J._M._Barrie "J. M. Barrie")'s *[Peter and Wendy](/wiki/Peter_and_Wendy "Peter and Wendy")*, this trio of songs follow [Wendy Darling](/wiki/Wendy_Darling "Wendy Darling") in her career as a [pirate](/wiki/Pirate "Pirate"). Taken from the album *Sirens*, the Wendy Trilogy assumes that Wendy took [Captain Hook](/wiki/Captain_Hook "Captain Hook") up on his offer to turn [buccaneer](/wiki/Buccaneer "Buccaneer"). The saga sees Wendy join the crew, grow strong, fall out with [Peter Pan](/wiki/Peter_Pan_(character) "Peter Pan (character)"), and finally attack Hook after hearing him plot to marry her off to [Bluebeard](/wiki/Bluebeard "Bluebeard"). In the ensuing fight, Peter drops Hook into the waiting mouth of the [crocodile](/wiki/Peter_and_Wendy#Crocodile "Peter and Wendy")–a coincidental fate, given that the trilogy shares an album with the song "Alligator in the House" (see below). From there, Wendy takes command of the pirate ship, trains an all-girl crew, and becomes a [Fairyland](/wiki/Fairy "Fairy") celebrity. Eventually she retires, handing the captaincy off to first mate Green-Eyed Sue. An illustration by artist Amy Brown was made for a single release of the Wendy Trilogy – yet to be released as a full EP of pirate songs entitled Pirate Girls, and "Alligator in the House" also appears on the album *Tricky Pixie–Live!* and *Mythcreants*, releases from Tucker's West Coast project [Tricky Pixie](/w/index.php?title=Tricky_Pixie&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tricky Pixie (page does not exist)").

## Tricky Pixie

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=S._J._Tucker&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Tricky Pixie")]

Founded in 2006, Tricky Pixie combines music and songwriting from Tucker, [Betsy Tinney](/w/index.php?title=Betsy_Tinney&action=edit&redlink=1 "Betsy Tinney (page does not exist)") of [Gaia Consort](/wiki/Gaia_Consort "Gaia Consort") and [Alexander James Adams](/wiki/Alexander_James_Adams "Alexander James Adams")–formerly [folk](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") artist [Heather Alexander](/wiki/Heather_Alexander "Heather Alexander"). Tinney and Tucker also collaborated on several other songs, including one of Tucker's "signature songs," "Alligator in the House". The band made their debut at [Soulfood Books](/w/index.php?title=Soulfood_Books&action=edit&redlink=1 "Soulfood Books (page does not exist)") in Redmond, WA. The recording of this first performance would become the *Tricky Pixie – Live!* album. The band also appeared–with [Celia](/w/index.php?title=Celia_(musician)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Celia (musician) (page does not exist)") – at the 2007 [Faerieworlds](/wiki/Faerieworlds "Faerieworlds") festival,[[18]](#cite_note-18) but due to the divergent careers of the three band members, as of 2008 Tricky Pixie performs only in the winter and summer months.[[19]](#cite_note-19)
